2001-01-01.title: Arafat Heads to Washington for Talks With Clinton; Car Bomb Hurts Dozens
2001-01-01.abstract: JERUSALEM, Jan. 1 — As hopes dimmed for a breakthrough on the peace front, a car bomb rocked the commercial heart of the central Israeli coastal city of Netanya tonight, wounding 30 people.

2001-01-01.title: Soft-Spoken Heir to Role Of Anti-Arab Militant
2001-01-01.abstract: Binyamin Kahane, soft-spoken and introverted, nevertheless took up his slain father's mantle, promoting Rabbi Meir Kahane's militant ideas in lectures, writings and political activity (M)

2001-01-01.title: Canada's Tribal Women Fight (Mostly Male) Graft
2001-01-01.abstract: New movement largely made up of Indian women take on Canada's native establishment, accusing them of rigging elections, stealing government money and living life of luxury while their people live in third world poverty; unchecked corruption and nepotism has pushed these women to violate central tenet of minority group politics: breaking ranks when dealing with white majority; women are demanding accountability for equivalent of $4 billion American that is spent on Canada's one million indigenous people; women contend that federal government's system of sending money through tribal chiefs has spawned corruption on reserves; phto; map (M)

2001-01-01.title: Intipuca Journal; Gaining Dollars, Town Is Losing Its Folkways
2001-01-01.abstract: El Salvador has adopted dollar as its official currency, beginning Jan 1; town of Intipuca has been driven by dollars for more than 20 years, the result of especially large migration to United States and many dollars sent back; for every one of town's 7,200 people, nearly two others have been lured to United States to toil in restaurants, factories and construction sites; emigre dollars have helped country amass significant dollar reserves, but some critics say search for dollar is exacting cost from their society; photo; map (M)

2001-01-01.title: Son of Slain Rabbi Kahane Dies With Wife in West Bank Ambush
2001-01-01.abstract: Binyamin Kahane, radical right-wing settler and son of assassinated Rabbi Meir Kahane, is himself killed along with his wife, Talia, in ambush shooting in West Bank; shooting attack also wounds five of Kahanes' six children; unknown Palestinian group, calling itself Martyrs of the Intifada, claims responsibility for attack; elder Kahane was killed in New York City decade ago; Israeli soldiers shoot and kill senior Palestinian health official, Dr Thabet Thabet, near his West Bank home; incident appears unrelated to Kahanes' shootings, but killings highlight fragility of moment, when time-bound American peace proposal is under active consideration; both Israeli and Palestinian groups opposed to Pres Clinton's peace proposal seize on attacks as further evidence of why it should be rejected; angry Palestinian demonstrators in West Bank as well as belligerent mourners for Kahanes call for swift revenge attacks; photos; map (M)

2001-01-02.title: Lord Aldington, 86, Libeled for 1945 Yalta Repatriations
2001-01-02.abstract: Lord Aldington, British politician and executive who won highest libel damages ever awarded by British court in his suit against a great-nephew of Russian novelist Leo Tolstoy, dies at age 86; photo (M)

2001-01-02.title: Queen Elizabeth National Park Journal; Grumpy and Big Eaters, Refugees Are Welcomed
2001-01-02.abstract: Uganda's Queen Elizabeth National Park has become haven for approximately 100 elephants fleeing civil war in neighboring Congo; animals have been welcomed in country where wildlife is only hope for reviving seriously slumping tourist trade; map; photos (M)

2001-01-02.title: Share Structure Gives Foreigners Better Deal : China Market Boom Is Raising Questions
2001-01-02.abstract: HONG KONG—  The development of China's stock markets has been dramatic, but these emerging markets badly need a more rational structure, and more equal valuations. Chinese stocks have outperformed most others in the past year, but that has only exacerbated a situation in which Chinese pay several times more than foreign investors for the same assets.

2001-01-02.title: Africa Diamond Hub Defies Smuggling Rules
2001-01-02.abstract: Sierra Leone remains bustling hub of Africa's diamond-smuggling trade, despite new UN rules to curb illicit activity that has sustained country's nine-year civil war; many traders say rules are so flawed that smuggling will never cease; say authorities lack resources to prevent smuggling, and have never shown will to reform corrupt system that has enriched successive governments; Sierra Leone stands more than ever as test of whether UN is serious about constraining illicit, $700-million-a-year business that has also aided ravaging of Congo and Angola; photos; maps (M)

2001-01-02.title: ARAFAT EXPECTED TO MEET CLINTON IN THE U.S. TODAY
2001-01-02.abstract: Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat leaves Gaza for hastily arranged meeting with Pres Clinton at White House to discuss reservations about American blueprint for final peace deal with Israel; announcement follows explosion of car bomb in Israeli coastal city of Netanya, wounding more than 30 people; Israel shuts down Palestinian airport in Gaza and tightens blockade of Palestinian areas; Palestinian officials express skepticism about last-minute peace deal, as do supporters in Arab world; map; photo (M)

2001-01-03.title: Cambodian Deputies Back War Crimes Court
2001-01-03.abstract: Cambodia's National Assembly, after months of delay, votes to create special tribunal, with help from UN, to try leaders of murderous Khmer Rouge regime of 1970's; officials and experts say many political and technical pitfalls still lie ahead; say it might be years before any of aging leaders face trial, and that some could evade justice altogether; photos (M)

2001-01-03.title: Not Museum Pieces, Yemeni Jews Do Their Best to Survive
2001-01-03.abstract: Article on plight of Yemen's small community of Jews which totals approximately 300 after waves of immigration; community is doing best to survive in hard place at time when tensions between Jews and Arabs are once again boiling into violence in Gaza Strip and West Bank; maps; photo (M)

2001-01-03.title: I.R.A. Asserts Onus Is Blair's In Peace Effort
2001-01-03.abstract: Irish Republican Army leaders issue New Year's statement reiterating pledge to resolve disarmament deadlock that has blocked progress in bringing peace to Northern Ireland, but say next steps must come from Prime Min Tony Blair; offer no encouragement that there will be any imminent moves; say they have met all commitments (M)

2001-01-03.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2001-01-03.abstract: ASIA    CHINA: FALUN GONG FATALITIES -- December was a deadly month for Falun Gong followers in China, where four more adherents of the spiritual discipline have died in confrontations with the police, a Hong Kong rights group said. Falun Gong followers say more than 90 members have died at the hands of the police since China banned the group and began a campaign of suppression in July 1999.    Craig S. Smith (NYT)

2001-01-03.title: Banker Tells Of Cover-Up On Account For Estrada
2001-01-03.abstract: MANILA—  A bank executive testifying in the impeachment trial of President Joseph Estrada said Tuesday that she had been ordered to cover up his alleged ownership of a multimillion-dollar secret bank account.

2001-01-04.title: Neither Side Is Game to Block Mideast Peace
2001-01-04.abstract: JERUSALEM, Jan. 4 — For a land where racquet sports are rare, tennis metaphors certainly get a workout. A key theme in the peace negotiations concerns whether the ball is in the Israelis' court or the Palestinians'.

2001-01-04.title: Out of Africa or Bust, With a Desert to Cross
2001-01-04.abstract: Dirkou, Niger, last sub-Saharan stop on one of Africa's greatest, and possibly most dangerous, smuggling routes, lures Africans who travel hundreds of miles to desert outpost in hopes of boarding Libyan trucks to take them across remaining expanses of Sahara into North Africa; migrants then stay in North Africa to work, or save up for jump to Europe and even America; exodus has resumed after two-month lull that followed Libyan mob attacks on African expatriates and expulsion of thousands into desert by planeload to Nigeria and Ghana; quick resumption reflects Africa's steep and general decline in recent years; map; photo (M)

2001-01-04.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2001-01-04.abstract: EUROPE    VATICAN: POPE AND PARKINSON'S  The orthopedic surgeon who operated on John Paul II in 1994 said he was concerned about the pope's health and listed Parkinson's disease as one of the pope's ailments. ''I should order him to rest but it would be futile,'' Gianfranco Fineschi said in remarks to the Italian weekly Oggi that were given in advance to the Italian news agency ANSA. The Vatican has never publicly confirmed the pope has Parkinson's.    Alessandra Stanley (NYT)

2001-01-04.title: Official Japan Does Musical Chairs, and Desks
2001-01-04.abstract: Japanese goverment is in throes of massive reorganization; 23 ministries and agencies are set to consolidate into 13 in bid to streamline nation's powerful bureaucracy and weaken its grip on Japanese life; reform involves 540,000 officials; bureaucrats are expected to continue to dominate policy, despite bid to shift power to politicians; photo (M)

2001-01-04.title: 50,000 Czechs Rally for Striking TV Journalists
2001-01-04.abstract: At least 50,000 Czechs rally, Prague, in support of striking television journalists who say they are trying to preserve their independence from political pressure; many score what they see as blatant effort by two big parties to control state-owned Czech Television; some 50 journalists are barricaded in newsroom in protest against appointment of Jiri Hodac, ally of political leader Vaclav Klaus, as general manager of station (M)

2001-01-05.title: Banned Chinese Sect Is Spurred On by Exiled Leader
2001-01-05.abstract: Civil disobedience by Chinese spiritual movement Falun Gong shows no sign of slowing in New Year and may be ratcheting up to new level; New Year's Day message to followers from movement's exiled founder, Li Hongzhi, posted on group's official Web site, warns that Falun Gong followers facing persecution could rightfully 'go beyond the limits of forbearance'; China's efforts to crush movement have reduced its numbers, but have also hardened resolve of those who remain loyal to Li; photo of Falun Gong followers being arrested by police in Tiananmen Square on New Year's Day (M)

2001-01-05.title: Dare From Palestinians
2001-01-05.abstract: News analysis: Palestinians have dared Israelis to return to negotiating table, despite significant gaps between sides, rather than be labeled roadblock to peace; leading Palestinian newspaper says Palestinians hope their 'acceptance' of Pres Clinton's proposal will prevent Israeli negotiatiors from holding Palestinian side responsible for possible collapse of peace process; now it is Israel's turn to seek clarifications on peace proposal from Americans, to suggest that other side is bluffing and to seek way to shift attention to Palestinians; getting or keeping world opinion on their side is as important to Israelis and Palestinians now as more elusive goal of securing peace deal; photo (M)

2001-01-05.title: Terrific News in Mexico City: Air Is Sometimes Breathable
2001-01-05.abstract: Mexico City's air, called worst in world by United Nations in 1992, is getting fresher--or at least less foul; Dr Mario J Molina, Nobel Prize winner in chemistry who has lead most intensive study of problem yet undertaken, says there are data suggesting that air may be improving; notes there has been no smog alert for 15 months, and that amount of lead, carbon dioxide and sulfur dioxide in air is down; other pollution levels are still often unacceptably high, but on many days, especially weekends, air is sometimes clear enough to breathe with pleasure, in city where foul air contributed to deaths of hundreds of people in one week four years ago; improving air is result of increasingly stringent environmental rules, which call for cleaner fuels, catalytic converters on cars, emissions tests and rules on industry; photos (M)

2001-01-05.title: REPORT TO CLINTON ASKS U.S. TO RATIFY TEST-BAN TREATY
2001-01-05.abstract: Gen John M Shalikashvili, former chairman of Joint Chiefs of Staff, who conducted comprehensive study of nuclear test ban treaty at request of Pres Clinton, concludes that United States must ratify it in order to mount effective campaign against spread of nuclear weapons; his assessment is part of last-ditch attempt by Clinton to build support for treaty, which Senate Republicans rejected in 1999 and on which Pres-elect George W Bush's own top aides have sharply disagreed; Bush assailed treaty as unverifiable and unenforceable during presidential campaign, but he promised to continue Clinton administration's moratorium on nuclear testing for time being; some Republican lawmakers say they might reconsider if treaty is modified or accompanied by new safeguards and if new Republican administration supports it; Shalikashvili's report outlines measures intended to assuage critics of treaty (M)

2001-01-05.title: British Doctor May Have Killed Hundreds
2001-01-05.abstract: LONDON, Jan. 5 — When Harold Shipman, the trusted family doctor in a village outside Manchester, was arrested in 1998 on suspicion of killing elderly women in his own care, such was the shock that hand-written notices from his patients went up in his main street storefront office saying, "We don't believe it."

2001-01-06.title: Kim Dae Jung Wants Bush Team's Support : Seoul to Make Its Case For 'Sunshine' Policy
2001-01-06.abstract: SEOUL—  President Kim Dae Jung said Friday that he expected to go to Washington soon after President-elect George W. Bush is inaugurated for a summit meeting in which he will try to win the new administration's support for his "sunshine" policy of reconciliation with North Korea.

2001-01-06.title: ISRAELI SEES TIME LACKING FOR PACT
2001-01-06.abstract: Gilead Sher, senior Israeli negotiator, says time has run for a full negotiated settlement with Palestinians during Clinton administration and that the best that can be expected will be general statement by Pres Clinton setting a path for future; White House will decide in next several days whether to keep struggling with possibility for negotiations to establish Palestinian state and to resolve future of Jerusalem and fate of Palestinian refugees (M)

2001-01-06.title: Change Is in the Air as Thailand Prepares to Vote
2001-01-06.abstract: Thailand prepares to vote for new Parliament under strict new rules to combat corruption and vote-buying; plenty of cash is still being handed out, but it is mostly hidden; election is latest and fiercest battleground in Thailand's slow, hard-fought democratic revolution, which some see as test for peaceful reform in South Asia; photo (M)

2001-01-06.title: U.S. Embassy In Rome Sends Staff Home After Threat
2001-01-06.abstract: US Embassy in Rome sends most of its employees home after learning of threat from Islamic terrorists, reportedly from people associated with Osama bin Laden's organization, Al Qaeda (S)

2001-01-06.title: Repair of 2nd Jet at Issue in Concorde Crash
2001-01-06.abstract: French investigators say most likely reason for crash of Air France Concorde outside Paris in July 2000 was piece of metal that fell from Continental plane that took off five minutes before it on flight to US; Air France suit against Continental alleges shortcomings in repairs on plane (S)

2001-01-07.title: Radiation From Balkan Bombing Alarms Europe
2001-01-07.abstract: Discovery of low-level radiation at 8 of 11 sites visited by United Nations panel sends sense of panic and fury throughout Europe about well-being of soldiers sent to serve in Balkans, more than 12 of whom have died of leukemia; residents of contaminated areas resent that they did not know until now of need to clean up low-level uranium dispersed by American weapons dropped over Bosnia and Yugoslavia; UN panel recommends contaminated areas be quarantined until cleanup is complete; photo; map (M)

2001-01-07.title: Romania's AIDS Children: A Lifeline Lost
2001-01-07.abstract: Hundreds of Romanian children who contracted AIDS virus ten years ago are at risk of dying because they can no longer get medication they need; high prices charged by Western drug companies combined with deep poverty keep drugs out of reach for hospitals financially crippled by recent bungling of national health budget; American and European drug companies have declined Romania's pleas for cheaper drugs and government has been unable to force prices down; donations of medicine and money are sporadic; children with AIDS are outcast in hospitals and often live in small apartment orphanages bedcause their families cannot care for them or afford drug therapies; photos; map (M)

2001-01-07.title: Putin Takes Up Debt and Defenses With German Chief
2001-01-07.abstract: Meeting between Pres Vladimir Putin and Chancellor Gerhard Schroder reflects close ties between Russia and Germany; issues of security, Russian debt and NATO expansion are on agenda (M)

2001-01-07.title: South Africa Asks for Help in Cholera Outbreak; Thousands Are Ill and 59 Are Dead
2001-01-07.abstract: South Africa enlists help from World Health Organization in stemming outbreak of cholera that has stricken more than 15,000 pople, killing 59 (M)

2001-01-07.title: George Carman, a Whiz at Libel Lawsuits in Britain, Dies at 71
2001-01-07.abstract: LONDON, Jan. 6 — George Carman, a libel lawyer who became a courtroom legend for his ability to woo juries with wit and warmth while doing in opponents with one- liners and devastating 11th-hour disclosures, died of prostate cancer Tuesday at his home in Wimbledon. He was 71 and had retired on his doctor's advice in August after 47 years at the British bar.

2001-01-08.title: DOUBTFUL ON PACT, CLINTON LAYS OUT PLAN FOR MIDEAST
2001-01-08.abstract: Pres Clinton delivers farewell address to Israel Policy Forum in New York City, spelling out for first time in plainest language yet his specific proposals to solve the Israeli-Palestinian conflict and acknowledging that final settlement is highly unlikely in final days of his term; says both sides need to make deep compromises and that there can be no genuine resolution to conflict without a sovereign and viable Palestinian state; says Israel's security requirements and demographic realities must be addressed; says to achieve Palestinian state goal should be to maximize number of settlers in Israel while minimizing land annexed; says for Palestine to be viable, it must be geographically contiguous state; says Jerusalem should be open and undivided city; other major points of speech discussed (M)

2001-01-08.title: Taiwan Is Likely to Ask the U.S. to Sell It 4 Destroyers
2001-01-08.abstract: Taiwan signals it may ask incoming Bush administration to sell it four Kidd-class guided-missile destroyers to counter China's recently purchased Russian-made destroyers; request would present administration with first major challenge in managing delicate balance of relations between China and Taiwan; under Taiwan Relations Act of 1979, US is obligated to help Taiwan maintain its defenses; Pres-elect George Bush has indicated in past that he will be more aggressive in fulfilling obligation than Clinton administration, which declined to sell Taiwan ships in 2000; photo (M)

2001-01-08.title: Modern Marketing Blooms at the Vatican Library
2001-01-08.abstract: Vatican Library, until recently highly restricted scholarly repository, expanded and refined merchandizing techniques during Holy Year Jubilee in 2000 and realized windfall from sale of reproductions to public via licensing agreement with California company, 1451 International Inc; Pope John Paul II says windfall will go to charity; photos (M)

2001-01-08.title: Navy Inquiry on Cole Urges No Punishment of Captain or Crew
2001-01-08.abstract: Navy commanders overseeing an investigation of terrorist suicide attack on destroyer Cole in Yemen in October 2000 reportedly recommend that neither ship's captain, Comdr Kirk S Lippold, nor crew members be punished, even though they failed to follow certain security guidelines; overturn initial findings of investigative officer who concluded effects of attack, which killed 17 sailors and wounded 39, might have been mitigated if Lippold had adhered to security measures (M)

2001-01-08.title: Ecuador Is Fearful As Colombia's War Spills Over Border
2001-01-08.abstract: Colombian conflict between right-wing paramilitary fighters and leftist guerrillas spills over into Ecuador in dingy Amazon border town of Lago Agrio; refugees fleeing intensifying combat in southern Colombia are also showing up, and affluent Colombians with no ties to area are suddenly buying up land and stocking up on chemicals used to process cocaine; Eucador, of all of Colombia's neighbors, is perhaps most vulnerable, least prepared and worst equipped to deal with such developments; rising violence in Lago Agrio, service center for oil industry, discussed; map; photo (M)

2001-01-09.title: Cole Panel Urges U.S. Military to Increase Troop Protection
2001-01-09.abstract: WASHINGTON, Jan. 9 — The United States military needs to devote much more time and money to combat the threat of terrorist attacks, a Pentagon commission that has been investigating the attack on the destroyer Cole said today.

2001-01-09.title: Punta del Este Journal; As Elite Resort Slumps, Argentina Doesn't Cry
2001-01-09.abstract: Punta del Este, resort area in Uruguay known throughout Latin America as playground of Argentine rich and of European royalty, is in slump because of two-year-old recession gripping Argentina; crisis is product of long-term social trends as well as five years of generally mediocre economic performance in Argentina; wealthy and upper-middle-class Argentine families once took month of two off in Punta as matter of recreation and prestige, but currently take shorter vacations; with overvalued peso pegged to strong dollar, they increasingly go to Europe, US and Brazil; map; photo (M)

2001-01-09.title: China Says Protest Papers Are Distorted
2001-01-09.abstract: Chinese Government defends crushing of pro-democracy demonstrations at Tiananmen Square in 1989 as highly necessary to stability and development of country in its first official response to 'Tiananmen papers'--reportedly internal documents newly published in US that show divisions within leadership at time; government spokesman also suggests papers are fake (S)

2001-01-09.title: Former Bosnian Serb Leader to Cooperate With Tribunal
2001-01-09.abstract: UNITED NATIONS, Jan. 9 — The former president of the Bosnian Serb republic was reported today to have flown to the Hague to cooperate with the United Nations war crimes tribunal for the former Yugoslavia, but her exact whereabouts were shrouded in mystery.

2001-01-09.title: Charles Helou, 88, President Of Lebanon in Tumult of 60's
2001-01-09.abstract: Charles Helou, who was Lebanon's president and head of state from 1964 to 1970, dies at age 88; photo (M)

2001-01-10.title: Ex-Bosnian Serb Chief Is Said to Surrender
2001-01-10.abstract: Biljana Plavsic, former president of Bosnian Serb republic, reportedly has flown to The Hague to cooperate with UN war crimes tribunal; her hard-line nationalist stance during Bosnian war has led to speculation that sealed indictment against her has been drawn up (S)

2001-01-10.title: Lesser Counts Are Dropped In Bombing At Lockerbie
2001-01-10.abstract: Chief prosecutor in Lockerbie trial drops two lesser counts against Libyan defendants in destruction of Pan Am Flight 103, and tries to convince three-judge jury that he has proved murder charge; Alistair P Campbell gives summation after defense abruptly drops plan to call series of witnesses (M)

2001-01-10.title: U.S. Preparing to End Ban and Approve Sale of F-16's to Chile
2001-01-10.abstract: Outgoing Clinton administration prepares to approve sale of F-16 fighter jets to Chile, ending ban of more than two decades on sale of sophisticated American weapons in Latin America (S)

2001-01-10.title: U.S. Envoy Will Try to Forge a Common Position, Israeli Says
2001-01-10.abstract: Israeli Prime Min Ehud Barak says American envoy Dennis Ross will seek common ground for statement on peace negotiations that could be issued by Pres Clinton before he leaves office; Palestinians issue position paper rejecting option of signing broad declaration of principles; also disagree with Israeli report that two sides have accepted security plan drawn up by CIA Dir George J Tenet (M)

2001-01-10.title: Two Germans Quit Cabinet For Handling Of Beef Scare
2001-01-10.abstract: German health and agriculture ministers, Andrea Fischer and Karl-Heinz Funke, resign over handling of mad cow disease outbreak that has shattered consumer confidence in beef, including country's beloved sausages; their photos (M)

2001-01-11.title: Lockerbie Prosecutor Ends Case With Discussion of Weak Point
2001-01-11.abstract: Alistair P Campbell, chief prosecutor in Lockerbie case, ends his summation of case against two Libyan defendants charged with blowing up Pan American Flight 103, which went down over Lockerbie, Scotland, killing 270 people in 1988; John Grant, leader of defense team, then sums up case's weaknesses (M)

2001-01-11.title: TWA's Farewell Will Radically Alter U.S. Airline Market
2001-01-11.abstract: WASHINGTON—  The plan of American Airlines' parent group to acquire the critically ailing Trans World Airlines Inc. and certain US Airways assets, confirmed Wednesday, would give American control of one-fourth of the U.S. air travel market and expand its reach abroad.

2001-01-11.title: Israeli and Palestinian Officials Meet
2001-01-11.abstract: JERUSALEM, Jan. 11 — After Israel took limited steps today to ease its blockade of Palestinian territories, senior officials from both sides met late tonight in the latest burst of activity on the deeply troubled peace front.

2001-01-11.title: Uruguay's Chief Is Frank (and Frankly Insulting)
2001-01-11.abstract: Pres Jorge Batlle of Uruguay is first leader of Latin American country to call for legalization of drugs; says legalization would lower drug prices and cut into profits of cartels, and would solve Colombian civil war; Battle's ideas are not likely to carry much weight beyond tiny country he rules, but he stands out in continent where weak, uncreative leadership hasbecome rule; photo (M)

2001-01-11.title: In France, the Wink at Corruption Gives Way to Scrutiny
2001-01-11.abstract: New generation of French prosecutors is boldly opening dozens of inquiries and pressing hard for evidence they want, after decades in which allegations of corruption among public officials were rarely pursued with much vigor; public officials have been implicated on all sides of political spectrum, with investigating magistrates searching ministers' homes and carting even most elite--including son of former Pres Francois Mitterrand--off to jail for questioning; some in France hope country is on cusp of thorough housecleaning of privileged political class; others point out there is still long way to go from aggressive investigations to convictions; photos (M)

2001-01-12.title: Lockerbie Defense Says Prosecution's Version Is Unbelievable
2001-01-12.abstract: Lawyer for Libyan defendant in Lockerbie trial tells jury that no one would be so foolhardy as to attempt bombing plot outlined by prosecution; defense strategy is to blame Palestinians in explosion that killed 259 people on board Pan Am jet and 11 on ground (M)

2001-01-12.title: Tears, Lies and Glimpses of Truth Keep Nigerians Glued to the TV
2001-01-12.abstract: LAGOS, Nigeria, Jan. 12 — Not since the soap opera "The Rich Also Cry" has television gripped Nigerians as this courtroom drama has. The poor cluster each night around the lone television at the neighborhood cafe; the rich retreat to their gated mansions and glue themselves to their giant sets.

2001-01-12.title: Mexico's Green Dream: No More Cancuns
2001-01-12.abstract: Mexico may for first time seek to limit new construction on still undeveloped sections of Caribbean coast, perhaps putting end to huge new resorts and environmental havoc they help create; aim is to prevent another Cancun, which has nearly 25,000 hotel rooms, roughly three million visitors a year and nowhere left to build; price of rapid construction has been slow destruction of beauty that drew people there in first place; Pres Vicente Fox promised in his inaugural address to build 'Mexico with an environmental conscience,' and he put Victor Lichtinger, new environment and natural resources minister, in charge; Lichtinger's test case is at Xcacel, beach 67 miles south of Cancun, which is one of world's last unsullied sanctuaries for green and loggerhead sea turtles, both endangered species; environmentalists have sued Spanish development company that bought beach for a song in deal brokered by now-fugitive governor of Quintana Roo, and they are calling on government to ban development; tourism is mother lode for Mexico, generating $8 billion yearly; photo; maps (M)

2001-01-12.title: Mitterrand's Son Free on Bail After 21 Days
2001-01-12.abstract: Jean-Christophe Mitterrand, 54-year-old son of former Pres Francois Mitterrand, is freed on bail after spending 21 days in jail on suspicion of being involved in illegal arms sale; he has denied any wrongdoing; claims investigators are using him to score political points and blacken his family name (M)

2001-01-12.title: After Slaughter of Ireland's Cattle, a Severe Disposal Problem
2001-01-12.abstract: European Union regulations, prompted by re-emergence of mad cow disease late last year, force farmers across Continent to test or detroy all cows more than 30 months old; Irish agriculture officials worry about what to do with bodies of slaughtered cattle; about 300,000 are expected to be destroyed by June (M)

2001-01-13.title: Hong Kong's No. 2 Official Suddenly Quits Her Post
2001-01-13.abstract: Anson Chan suddenly resigns as Hong Kong's second-highest official, head of civil service; was widely viewed as symbol of former colony's determination to retain measure of autonomy under Chinese rule; critics of Chinese government say her departure raises fresh questions about Beijing's role in running Hong Kong; photo (M)

2001-01-13.title: Mideast Clock Ticks, Perhaps Inaudibly to Some
2001-01-13.abstract: Israeli and Palestinian negotiators dwell on side issues rather than future of Jerusalem and Palestinian refugees even though Pres Clinton's blueprint for a final status peace agreement is set to expire when Clinton leaves office on Jan 20; Clinton administration officials try to inject sense of urgency, particularly in Palestinians, who are perceived as the reluctant ones, reminding them that incoming Bush administration will have a lot to tackle and that close, sometimes nearly daily mediation efforts of Clinton years are not likely to continue; Americans at least hope to stabilize violent conflict, and they see some positive signs on the ground; photos (M)

2001-01-13.title: Fray in Europe Over Uranium Draws Doubters
2001-01-13.abstract: Physicists and medical experts raise doubts about allegations that some allied troops could have contracted leukemia from exposure to depleted uranium used in NATO ammunition in Balkans and that civilians were put at risk by military testing; among skeptics are Dr Frank von Hippel of Princeton, Dr Michael Thun of American Cancer Society, Dr Charles Phelps of University of Rochester, Dr John Boice of International Epidemiology Institute and Dr Bruce Boecker of Lovelace Repiratory Research Instiutute; photo (M)

2001-01-13.title: Tears, Lies and Glimpses of Truth Keep Nigerians Glued to the TV
2001-01-13.abstract: Broadcasts of hearings of Nigeria's Human Rights Violations Investigation Commission keep Nigerians glued to their television sets; commission, set up by Prs Olusegun Obasanjo and intended as catharsis to the end of brutal dictatorship of late Gen Sani Abacha, have yielded high drama in form of plots and counterplots, talk of poison flasks with secret compartments and lethal chemical sprinkled on eyeglasses; many witnesses are believed to have lied in court to protect themselves, but tantalizing glimpses of truth have emerged, notably regarding death in 1998 of the opposition leader Moshood K O Abiola and Nigeria's subsequent swift transition to civilian rule; photo; map (M)

2001-01-13.title: One Man and a Global Web of Violence
2001-01-13.abstract: In 1987, several years after he began training Arab volunteers to oust Soviet forces from Afghanistan, Osama bin Laden had a vision. The time had come, he told friends, to start a global jihad, or Islamic holy war, against the corrupt secular governments of the Muslim Middle East and the Western powers that supported them.

2001-01-14.title: A Foiled Plot by Terrorists Was Aimed at Hundreds of Victims
2001-01-14.abstract: After weeks of advanced training in explosives at one of Osama bin Laden's Afghan camps, Raed Hijazi, a onetime Boston cab driver, was ready for his mission. The chemicals for the explosives were stockpiled. The targets were selected, the homemade detonators wired.

2001-01-14.title: Only Joking. But Healing South Africa, Too.
2001-01-14.abstract: Young black, Indian and mixed-race comedians are appearing in clubs and on television in South Africa, giving whites an intimate glimpse of their joys and frustrations and using laughter as bridge in a divided society; address issues that are usually avoided in day-to-day conversation between whites and blacks: lingering racial sterotypes, ugly tensions between lighter and darker-skinned people and struggle of the newly empowered in post-apartheid era; among the more prominent are Marc Lottering, Kagiso Lediga, Riaad Moosa and Kurt Schoonraad; photos (M)

2001-01-14.title: One Man and a Global Web of Violence
2001-01-14.abstract: First of three articles on Osama bin Laden and the creation of Al Qaeda, Muslim terrorist group he founded 13 years ago; group's initial target was Soviet forces occupying Afghanistan, but today its enemy is United States, which it regards as an imperial power propping up corrupt and godless governments; chronology; photos (L)

2001-01-14.title: Prague Trying To Sort Out TV Politics
2001-01-14.abstract: Lower chamber of Czech Parliament dismisses council that oversees Czech Television and provoked crisis in December by appointing Jiri Hodac as its director; moves are not sufficient to please journalists occupying the station (M)

2001-01-14.title: HOLY WARRIORS -- First of three articles: A Network of Terror; One Man and a Global Web of Violence
2001-01-14.abstract: First of three articles on Osama bin Laden and the creation of Al Qaeda, Muslim terrorist group he founded 13 years ago; group's initial target was Soviet forces occupying Afghanistan, but today its enemy is United States, which it regards as an imperial power propping up corrupt and godless governments; chronology; photos (L)

2001-01-15.title: On Jordan's Death Row, Convicted Terrorist Says He Has No Regrets
Big Tobacco Said to Fight Swiss Smoking Laws
Moscow 'Spy' Case Is Still a Mystery
2001-01-15.abstract: Edmond D Pope, pardoned by Russian Pres Vladimir V Putin after being convicted of espionage, hails efforts of Rep John E Peterson and his wife, Cheri, to win his release, phone interview; Peterson says he is unsure whether Pope spied, noting military and Central Intelligence Agency said he did not; uncertainty still surrounds United States claims that Pope was innocent businessman and that he suffers from rare cancer that probably worsened in prison; Pope says he was not mistreated and that cancer remains in remission; photo (M)

2001-01-15.title: Top German Official Dogged by 70's Role
2001-01-15.abstract: Germany's foreign minister, Joschka Fischer, finds himself under fire over newly published photographs showing him in his years of leftist agitation hitting a policeman during a 1970's demonstration.

2001-01-15.title: Dissecting a Terror Plot From Boston to Amman
2001-01-15.abstract: Second of three articles in series Holy Warriors, on global Islamic terrorism, focuses on foiled plot in Jordan, backed by Osama bin Laden, to kill hundreds of Americans, Israelis and other visitors marking dawn of year 2000; officials call plot case study of how bin Laden and his deputies, isolated in Afghanistan, have greatly extended their reach by aiding locally initiated terrorism; say Raed Hijazi, onetime Boston cabdriver of Palestinian origin being retried in case in Jordan, has given them new data on plot and its ties to Osama's Al Qaeda; maps; photos (L)

2001-01-16.title: Taiwan's Top Court Decides Halting A-Plant Was Improper
2001-01-16.abstract: Taiwan's high court rules government of Pres Chen Shui-bian acted improperly in halting building of nuclear power plant begun by his Nationalist predecessors; says government should have sought approval of Nationalist-controlled legislature; $5.5 billion project has come to symbolize rift between long-dominant Nationalists and Chen's party, which ousted them from presidency in 2000 (M)

2001-01-16.title: President of Congo Reportedly Shot
2001-01-16.abstract: UNITED NATIONS - The president of Congo, Laurent Kabila, was reportedly shot today in the capital, Kinshasa, according to diplomats and sketchy news reports, but whether he was alive was uncertain.

2001-01-16.title: Clinton Seeks to Keep Foreigners From Hiding Wealth in U.S.
2001-01-16.abstract: Clinton administration will announce new measures banks may take to deter foreign leaders and their associates from hiding money in US, leading destination for illicit wealth; voluntary guidelines are result of delicate compromise between leading banks and law enforcement officials, whose sharp disagreements in recent weeks nearly derailed administration's final anti-money-laundering initiative (M)

2001-01-16.title: German Foreign Minister Gives Testimony in Murder Trial
2001-01-16.abstract: FRANKFURT, Jan. 16 - In a courthouse here, Germany confronted a critical chapter of its postwar history today as Foreign Minister Joschka Fischer testified at the trial of an accused terrorist who was once Mr. Fischer's comrade in revolutionary struggle.

2001-01-16.title: Fujiyoshida Journal; View of Mount Fuji May Soon Include Fireworks
2001-01-16.abstract: Steep rise in low-frequency earthquakes around Mount Fuji in Fujiyoshida, Japan, may be precursor of eruption; has grabbed attention of earthquake and volcano experts and made central and local government officials uneasy; last violent eruption was in 1707; long period of dormancy has made many Japanese forget Fuji is still active; map; photo (M)

2001-01-17.title: 11 Are Killed as Militants Attack Kashmir Airport
2001-01-17.abstract: Militants waging guerrilla war against Indian rule in Kashmir attack airport in Srinagar killing 11 people; dead include six attackers and 3 police officers (S)

2001-01-17.title: Israeli Campaign Advertising Shifts Into High Gear on TV
2001-01-17.abstract: Israel's election campaign shifts into high gear with barrage of television commercials from incumbent, Prime Min Ehud Barak, and challenger, Ariel Sharon; Sharon, who leads Barak by commanding majority in polls, preaches healing of rifts and calls for unity government after election; Barak focuses on Sharon's performance as defense minister in 1982, when he led invasion that embroiled Israel in Lebanon for 18 years; photo (M)

2001-01-17.title: Congo Leader Reportedly Dead After Being Shot by Bodyguard
2001-01-17.abstract: Congo Pres Laurent Kabila is reportedly assassinated by bodyguard; shooting reportedly involved dispute between Kabila and some of his generals; government seals borders, closes airport and imposes night curfew; soldiers reportedly surround presidential palace; capital of Kinshasa appears calm; implications of Kabila's death discussed; assassination could dramatically alter dynamics of two-and-a-half-year war that has drawn in half dozen African nations and destabilized all of Central Africa; photo; map (M)

2001-01-17.title: A Mountain Village, Even More Sealed Off by the Quake
2001-01-17.abstract: 1,500 residents of Talnique, El Salvador, remain without public services in midst of almost total destruction caused by January 15 earthquake; photo (M)

2001-01-17.title: North Korean Leader and Entourage Make Furtive Trip to Shanghai
2001-01-17.abstract: North Korean leader Kim Jong Il, arrives, Shanghai, on furtive impromptu tour of China's economic capital; apparently catches Chinese bureaucracy by surprise (S)

2001-01-18.title: But Senate Democrats Assail His Views on Gun Control and Abortion : Ashcroft Vows to Back All Laws
2001-01-18.abstract: WASHINGTON—  Democratic senators on Wednesday continued a sometimes fierce questioning of John Ashcroft, the attorney general-designate, eliciting repeated assurances that he would vigorously enforce all U.S. laws, including some he strongly opposes, such as the constitutional right to abortion.

2001-01-18.title: Softball for Powell, and With No Sweat
2001-01-18.abstract: Gen Colin L Powell appears at his confirmation hearing before Senate Foreign Relations Committee; Powell, well-acquainted with old world of Soviet Union and its weapons, seeks to convince Senate inquisitors that he also knows new world of Internet and information revolution; Powell stresses repeatedly that Bush administration is determined to move forward with national missile defense plan, even in face of allied opposition; photo (M)

2001-01-18.title: Chihuahua State Governor Shot; Ex-Officer Is Detained in Attack
2001-01-18.abstract: Patricio Martinez Garcia, governor of Chihuahua, Mexico's largest state, is shot and wounded in assassination attempt; he is said to be in stable condition; spokesman for state attorney general's office announces arrest of Victoria Loya, police officer dismissed four years ago after being accused of trying to shoot her husband; Martinez had been regarded as potential presidential candidate in 2006 (M)

2001-01-18.title: Tel Aviv Journal; New Conflict Begets Culture War by Israeli Artists
2001-01-18.abstract: Israeli artists join forces for exhibit to denounce 33d year of Israeli occupation of West Bank and Gaza Strip; exhibit, in loft in Tel Aviv, features work of 70 artists who were asked to produce something specifically related to political situation; works will be sold to benefit Palestinian medical clinics; Israeli artists are usually reluctant to get politically involved, for fear that their artwork will be used as political propaganda; photo (M)

2001-01-18.title: Congo Says Leader Is Alive but Installs His Son in Top Role
2001-01-18.abstract: Congolese officials insist that Pres Laurent Kabila is alive, but foreign officials say Congolese want to put succession in place before acknowledging that Kabila was killed at presidential palace on Jan 16; Kabila's son, Joseph, assumes role of interim leader of government, which has been opposed by three rebel groups and two outside nations since Kabila took over in 1997; Kabila's enemies, aware that uncertainty could explode into greater violence, call for renewed commitment to peace accord signed in mid-1999--though not respected by any side; spokesman for Rwandan government says Kabili was obstacle to honoring peace accord; little is certain of events on Jan 16, other than reported gun battle that erupted between Pres Kabila and members of his armed forces inside presidential palace; it is not clear if battle was actual attempt at coup; fight may have resulted from argument between Kabila and his generals, who seem to have grown increasingly dissatisfied with him; photo (M)

2001-01-19.title: Officials Call Economy Down, But Not Out, in the Philippines
2001-01-19.abstract: Tens of thousands of anti-government protesters choke streets of Manila's business district; top advisers to Pres Joseph Estrada acknowledge that political turmoil is damaging economy of Philippines; contend that collapse of Estrada's impeachment trial will not propel country into recession (M)

2001-01-19.title: Sri Lanka Restarts Drive on Rebels at Crucial Pass
2001-01-19.abstract: Hopes that cease-fire by rebels in Sri Lanka would quickly revive a moribund peace effort seem to fade this week as government mounts major offensive to recapture a strategic spit of land (M)

2001-01-19.title: A Nervous Congo Admits That Its President Is Dead
2001-01-19.abstract: Congo government finally announces that Pres Laurent Kabila had died, two days after it was reported that he had been shot by a bodyguard; it is not clear that battle-worn and impoverished Congo is ready to accept his 31-year-old son as his successor; photo of son, Major Gen Joseph Kabila; map (L)

2001-01-19.title: Palestinians Trying to Root Out Informers
2001-01-19.abstract: Palestinian Authority is pushing campaign to root out traitors and informers, an effort that has led 'several hundred' Palestinians to turn themselves in; campaign began with executions last weekend of two 'collaborators' after their convictions in lightning trials for assisting Israel in assassinating Palestinian militants; photo (M)

2001-01-19.title: U.S. Twins in Britain Custody Fight
2001-01-19.abstract: LONDON, Jan. 19 — A trans-Atlantic struggle for custody of baby twins was headed for the courts today after they were taken from their British adoptive parents in a late night surprise visit by social service workers at a North Wales hotel where the family was staying.

2001-01-20.title: No Honeymoon Likely for New President's Foreign Policy Team
2001-01-20.abstract: Every president takes office believing he can set the foreign policy agenda for his administration, only to discover that a major portion of that agenda has already been set by the world.

2001-01-20.title: No Honeymoon Likely for New President's Foreign Policy Team
2001-01-20.abstract: Every president takes office believing he can set the foreign policy agenda for his administration, only to discover that a major portion of that agenda has already been set by the world.

2001-01-20.title: Israelis Grieve as Youth Who Was Lured to His Death on the Internet Is Buried
2001-01-20.abstract: Israelis grieve for Ofir Rahum, 16, who was murdered after being lured to Jerusalem to meet young woman with whom he had nurtured online relationship; photo; bullet-riddled and stabbed body was found in West Bank near Ramallah; Israelis link killing to Palestinian conflict, and believe youth was set up and ambushed (M)

2001-01-20.title: British Court Sets Deadline to Review Trans-Atlantic Adoption
2001-01-20.abstract: British court will hear trans-Atlantic adoption dispute after six-month-old twins are taken from British adoptive parents, Alan and Judith Kilshaw, by social service workers and placed in temporary foster care; Kilshaws say they adopted Missouri-born babies in Arkansas after paying $12,000 to broker in California whom they found on Internet; adoption is challenged by birth mother, Tranda Wecker, and by California couple, Vickie and Richard Allen, who had paid same broker, Tina Johnson, $6,000 and had twins for two months (M)

2001-01-20.title: No Honeymoon Likely for New President's Foreign Policy Team
2001-01-20.abstract: Every president takes office believing he can set the foreign policy agenda for his administration, only to discover that a major portion of that agenda has already been set by the world.

2001-01-21.title: Mad Cow Disease Is Claiming Some Political Victims, Too
2001-01-21.abstract: PARIS, Jan. 20 — Angry over the handling of the mad cow disease crisis, French farmers threw stones at their prime minister this week. Spanish cattle ranchers blockaded slaughterhouses to cut off the supply of meat. Other farmers refused to let health workers onto their property.

2001-01-21.title: Iraq Rebuilt Bombed Arms Plants, Officials Say
2001-01-21.abstract: WASHINGTON, Jan. 21 — Iraq has rebuilt a series of factories that the United States has long suspected of producing chemical and biological weapons, according to senior government officials. The new intelligence estimate could confront President Bush with an early test of his pledge to take a tougher stance against President Saddam Hussein than the Clinton administration did.

2001-01-21.title: Multiplying Albanian Insurgents in Yugoslavia Threaten Belgrade's New Democracy
2001-01-21.abstract: Growing strength of ethnic Albanian insurgents in Serbia, who want to annex parts of Serbia they would like to attach to an independent Kosovo, is becoming serious embarrassment to NATO-led peacekeeping force in Kosovo, and source of political instability to new democratic government in Belgrade; photo; map (M)

2001-01-21.title: North Korean Placed Focus On Business In China Visit
2001-01-21.abstract: North Korean leader, Kim Jong Il, returns home from secretive five-day visit to China after giving strongest signals yet that he hopes to begin opening his country's isolated, controlled economy to outside investment and market forces; Kim spends nearly all his visit, his second to China since May, touring companies and discussing economic issues in Shanghai, China's commercial hub (M)

2001-01-21.title: Israelis and Palestinians to Begin High-Level Talks in Egypt Today
2001-01-21.abstract: Israel and the Palestinians are set to begin intensive, high-level peace negotiations on Sunday evening in the Egyptian resort town of Taba, both sides announced tonight.    After four months of violent conflict, the negotiations will strive for much more than just the cessation of violence, which has been the goal of most Israeli-Palestinian contacts until very recently.

2001-01-22.title: 37 NEW CARDINALS SELECTED BY POPE; EGAN IS ELEVATED
2001-01-22.abstract: Pope John Paul II names 37 new cardinals, shaping future of Roman Catholic Church and institution that will someday elect his successor; record number includes 3 Americans and 10 from Latin America, deepening church's reach in developing world; Americans are archbishops Edward M Egan of New York and Theodore E McCarrick of Washington, who hold key posts in church's hierarchy, and Rev Avery Dulles, Jesuit theologian at Fordham University who has defended pope's policies against liberal critics; while nine appointees are from Curia in keeping with Vatican tradition, new selections reflect pope's desire to broaden church's reach particularly in third world; largest concentration of 10 new cardinals are from Latin America, which as bloc will have 26 voting cardinals; Italy will have single largest number, at 24; photos (M)

2001-01-22.title: Egan Calls Promotion Pope's Gift To Diocese
2001-01-22.abstract: Archbishop Edward M Egan of New York says he is deeply humbled by his appointment as cardinal by Pope John Paul II; says honor belongs first and foremost to archdiocese, which became highly influential within church and society under John Cardinal O'Connor, Terence Cardinal Cooke and Francis Cardinal Spellman (M)

2001-01-22.title: Roadblocks Right and Left for Mexican President
2001-01-22.abstract: Pres Vicente Fox, who took power at end of 2000 promising nothing less than second Mexican revolution, is encountering obstacles from left and right in country's new rough-and-tumble atmosphere of democracy; each of his first big proposals--to transform country's electric grid with help from abroad, rewrite tax code and guarantee rights for 10 million Indians--are likely to be blocked by powerful Institutional Revolutionary Party (PRI), which governed for 70 years, and left-leaning Party of Democratic Revolution; constitution virtually forbids foreign investment in electricity production, political and popular opposition to tax reform is fierce, and some members of PRI say granting even limited autonomy to Indians would be death of nation; photo (M)

2001-01-22.title: Colombian Paramilitaries Adjust Attack Strategies
2001-01-22.abstract: Colombia's surging paramilitary army, United Self-Defense Forces of Colombia, is waging fierce urban battle against leftist rebels in grimy northern industrial center of Barrancabermeja in conflict usually fought in countryside; has retooled for urban warfare, abruptly changing strategy in new environment; human rights groups say militia is killing fewer people than rebels, who have responded to threat in neighborhoods they have long controlled with furious assault on those they accuse of supporting paramilitaries; maps; photo (M)

2001-01-22.title: Upbeat Mood in Manila, and Vows for Future
2001-01-22.abstract: Upbeat mood sweeps across Manila, Philippines, with talk centering on pivotal role played by huge public demonstrations in forcing scandal-plagued Pres Joseph Estrada from office; his successor, Vice Pres Gloria Macapagal Arroyo, praises young Filipinos who made up vast majority of demonstrators and brought about resurgence of 'people power' that forced former Pres Ferdinand Marcos from office in 1986; pledges to work to give them bright future; Roman Catholic Cardinal Jaime Sin admonishes Filipinos to remain vigilant (M)

2001-01-23.title: Congo Buries Elder Kabila
2001-01-23.abstract: KINSHASA, Congo, Jan. 23 — President Laurent Kabila, who was gunned down in his office here a week ago, was buried here in the capital today in a state funeral rich with symbolism evoking the country's troubled past and present.

2001-01-23.title: As New Peace Talks Go On, Palestinians Criticize Clinton
2001-01-23.abstract: Israeli and Palestinian negotiators push ahead with what both sides say could be most serious and detailed effort yet to reach comprehensive peace agreement since failed Camp David meetings six months ago; Palestinians issue memorandum harshly criticizing Clinton administration's 'mistakes and failures' in earlier negotiations; suggest exit of Clinton administration is one of factors prompting new push; accuse Clinton diplomatic team of making peace process goal in and of itself and pursuing policy of 'constructive ambiguity' that left issues unresolved with disastrous consequences for region; additional factor in push for new talks is possibility that Prime Min Ehud Barak may lose February 6 election to Ariel Sharon, hawkish leader of Israeli opposition; photo (M)

2001-01-23.title: Australians Create a Deadly Mouse Virus
2001-01-23.abstract: Australian scientists accidentally create virus that kills mice by crippling their immune system during experiment to make rodents infertile; warn technique could produce deadlier forms of human viruses and new kinds of biological weapons; virus resulted after addition of gene involved in mouse immune system to mousepox virus--cousin to human smallpox virus widely used in lab studies; mice infected with pathogen died, even many of those vaccinated against mousepox; similar step could in theory create pathogen deadly to people since they have same immune system gene (M)

2001-01-23.title: Sect Members Immolate Selves in Tiananmen Square
2001-01-23.abstract: BEIJING, Jan. 23 — Five members of the banned Falun Gong spiritual group doused themselves with gasoline and set themselves on fire in the middle of Tiananmen Square this afternoon, a dramatic act of protest on the eve of China's most joyous holiday, Spring Festival or Lunar New Year.

2001-01-23.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2001-01-23.abstract: EUROPE    GERMANY: PAYING VICTIMS -- The government will compensate victims of far-right violence, Justice Minister Herta Daubler-Gmelin announced. The justice ministry will make $4.75 million available to victims of violence, threats or insults in a program that will apply retroactively to 1999. It will also allow payments to very serious cases from before then.    Victor Homola (NYT)

2001-01-24.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2001-01-24.abstract: EUROPE    TURKEY: FRENCH DEAL DITCHED  Angered by the French Parliament's passage of a bill declaring that the Ottoman Empire's killing of Armenians was genocide, the Turkish government canceled a $149 million deal to buy a spy satellite from the French firm Alcatel and excluded another French company from competing to sell              Turkey tanks worth up to $7 billion.    Douglas Frantz (NYT)

2001-01-24.title: A Rocky Meeting in Belgrade For War Crimes Prosecutor
2001-01-24.abstract: Carla del Ponte, chief prosecutor of United Nations war crimes tribunal in The Hague, meets with Pres Vojislav Kostunica of Yugoslavia in Belgrade, and they appear to disagree on many issues; del Ponte leaves in some haste without handing over a sealed indictment for someone living in Yugoslavia whom she hoped the government would help to arrest; has demanded that former Pres Slobodan Milosevic be arrested and transferred to The Hague, but Kostunica has made it clear that he is reluctant to cooperate and has repeatedly described tribunal as a political institution that is biased against Serbs; photo (M)

2001-01-24.title: Oil Spill's Shift in Course Aids Galapagos Mop-Up
2001-01-24.abstract: Oil spills now cover more than 775 square miles of pristine waters in the Galapagos Islands and continue to spread; Ecuador declares state of emergency and is stepping up its efforts to prevent the slicks from contaminating habitats of world's rarest marine and bird species; its handling of various aspects of crisis is criticized by environmental groups and local press; sudden and welcome wind shift is driving most of the 144,000 gallons of spilled oil away from inhabited areas; photo; map (M)

2001-01-24.title: Spreading Deforestation Is Both Acknowledged and Ignored : Europe's Decaying Ecosystem
2001-01-24.abstract: PARIS—  What is left of Europe's ancient forests, the source of so many myths and legends, is under threat from ax, fire, pollution and urban sprawl, and little is being done about it.

2001-01-24.title: Corporate Bonds Feel Heat As Company Defaults Rise
2001-01-24.abstract: NEW YORK—  CORRECTION PUBLISHED IN THE INTERNATIONAL HERALD TRIBUNE DATED January 30, 2001.

2001-01-25.title: Galapagos Islands Face New Peril as More Oil Spills From Tanker
2001-01-25.abstract: High tides and heavy winds force suspension of efforts to recover diesel and bunker fuel leaking from grounded tanker that has already spilled over 150,000 gallons into pristine waters off Galapagos Islands; Ecuador continues cleanup and animal-rescue efforts, aided by volunteers and United States Coast Guard team (M)

2001-01-25.title: Colombia Adds Troops at Rebel Zone as Deadline Nears
2001-01-25.abstract: Colombia has rushed hundreds of troops to area just outside huge rebel-held territory, as prospects of resuming peace talks by Jan 31 deadline with Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia near collapse; Pres Andres Pastrana is cutting short European trip to deal with crisis, which is hurting peso; will weigh effort to take back 16,000 square miles of jungles and ranches he ceded to rebels in Nov 1998; photo (M)

2001-01-25.title: Mideast Peace Talks Resume After Violence
2001-01-25.abstract: TABA, Egypt, Jan. 25 — After a 48-hour hiatus, Israeli and Palestinian negotiators resumed their peace talks here today, still hoping for a diplomatic breakthrough, but increasingly dubious about a full-fledged agreement before the Feb. 6 election.

2001-01-25.title: Bush Team Tiptoeing Around Mideast
2001-01-25.abstract: Bush administration is taking more subdued approach to latest round of Israeli-Palestinian talks, in contrast with Clinton Administration's frenetic dealings in its final months; State Department says Israeli Prime Min Ehud Barak and Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat have called Sec Colin L Powell to inform him about talks; says Bush awaits outcome of Israel's Feb election, while Powell has delegated much of work on talks in Egypt to his ambassadors in region (M)

2001-01-25.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2001-01-25.abstract: EUROPE    YUGOSLAVIA: PROSECUTOR GREETED BY EGGS -- Carla del Ponte, chief prosecutor of the United Nations war crimes tribunal in The Hague, was pelted with eggs thrown by supporters of the former president of Yugoslavia, Slobodan Milosevic, as she drove away from a meeting in Belgrade. The prosecutor met with Yugoslav cabinet ministers on the second day of her three-day visit to seek cooperation on war crimes prosecutions.    Carlotta Gall (NYT)

2001-01-26.title: Israeli Campaign Is Tepid Despite High Stakes
2001-01-26.abstract: JERUSALEM, Jan. 26 — When Israel and the Palestinians are simultaneously engaged in a violent conflict and peace negotiations, with the potential for their precarious relationship to disintegrate quickly, Israelis' choice of a leader could have far-reaching consequences.

2001-01-26.title: Cairo Journal; Egyptians Tighten a Seat-Belt Law Till It Hurts
2001-01-26.abstract: Cairo, Egypt, police start to strictly enforce seat-belt laws; photo (M)

2001-01-26.title: In Premier's Job, Where Quebec Lost an Orator, It Prepares to Gain an Accountant
2001-01-26.abstract: Profile of Bernard Landry, heir apparent to Quebec's departing premier, Lucien Bouchard; after five years of Bouchard's failed fireworks over Quebec independence, and after Bouchard's emotional resignation on Jan 11, separatist government is preparing for reign of Landry, Quebec's quiet, successful finance minister; Parti Quebecois has united in support of Landry; photo (M)

2001-01-26.title: Beijing in Battle With Sect: 'A Giant Fighting a Ghost'
2001-01-26.abstract: China's battle with Falun Gong, banned spiritual movement, discussed; Chinese government's 18-month campaign has failed to eliminate group, or even reliably identify its millions of quietly devoted followers who make it so resilient; brazen expressions of dissent are mounting; silent majority in Falun Gong do not demonstrate; yet they form an enormous reservoir of believers who may someday be driven to act; they provide one another with emotional and sometimes financial support--as they do those members who have been jailed or lost jobs; photo (L)

2001-01-26.title: Reformer Finally Confirmed As Serbia's Prime Minister
2001-01-26.abstract: Zoran Djindjic, leader of Democratic Party, is confirmed as Serbia's new prime minister, and his government is sworn in month after his 18-party alliance wins sweeping election campaign (M)

2001-01-27.title: Galápagos Journal; Isles Rich in Species Are Origin of Much Tension
2001-01-27.abstract: Ecuador's capacity to administer Galapagos Islands, home to some of world's rarest marine and bird species, is being called into question in aftermath of damaging oil spill; conservation groups say leakage of more than 150,000 gallons of fuel from oil tanker that ran aground on reef was accident waiting to happen; Ecuadorean government promises to tighten lax standards and even looser enforcement that made spill possible, but skepticism about that pledge is almost universal; photo (M)

2001-01-27.title: Street Style Leaders Are Dressing Up in Tweeds and Brogues : London Lads Go Up-Country
2001-01-27.abstract: LONDON—  London lad street style of late has been led not by fashion but by the film director Guy Ritchie; the man who married Madonna. With two London-based gangster movies under his belt, Ritchie is an icon of urban cool.

2001-01-27.title: Oliver R. Gurney, 86, Professor And Expert on Ancient Hittites
2001-01-27.abstract: Prof Oliver Robert Gurney, Oxford classicist and leading scholar of Hittites, died Jan 11 at age of 86 (M)

2001-01-27.title: India, in Grief and Anger, Tallies Huge Quake Toll
2001-01-27.abstract: AHMEDABAD, India, Jan. 27 — The floors of the collapsed school are stacked on top of each other like four thick slices of bread and crushed between them are dozens of the sons and daughters of India's striving middle class, trapped when a powerful earthquake struck this thriving city in western India on Friday.

2001-01-27.title: Mideast Talks End With Gain but No Accord
2001-01-27.abstract: JERUSALEM, Jan. 27 — Senior Israeli and Palestinian officials concluded nearly a week of stop-and- start negotiations in Egypt tonight by saying they had made substantial progress and could envision a final peace accord but did not have sufficient time to conclude one before the Israeli elections on Feb. 6.

2001-01-28.title: Details of Corruption Emerge in China
2001-01-28.abstract: Chinese government investigators aggressively pursue series of major corruption cases, some of which lead perilously close to leadership in Beijing; probably the most exposed of China's leaders is Li Peng, conservative head of Parliament, who has close ties to State Power Co and two of whose children have made fortunes as executives in the industry (M)

2001-01-28.title: Blair Confidant's Slip Gives an Old Party Rival Running Room
2001-01-28.abstract: Resignation of Peter Mandelson, closest confidant of Prime Min Tony Blair of Britain, places extraordinary powers with Chancellor of Exchequer Gordon Brown; Mandelson has been only man who challenged Brown for Blair's allegiance, but relationship between Blair and Brown has been marked by irritations (M)

2001-01-28.title: Mideast Talks End With Gain But No Accord
2001-01-28.abstract: Senior Israeli and Palestinian officials conclude nearly a week of stop-and-start negotiations in Taba, Egypt, by saying jointly that they they have 'never been closer to reaching' a final peace accord but lack sufficient time to conclude one before Israeli elections on Feb 6; Prime Min Ehud Barak of Israel and Yasir Arafat, Palestinian leader, may meet within days in Stockholm (M)

2001-01-28.title: India, in Grief and Anger, Tallies Huge Quake Toll
2001-01-28.abstract: Rescue workers continue desperate effort to find the living buried in rubble left by earthquake that hit western India on Jan 26; devastation is gravest around Bhuj; among the dead are many of high school students who were in school in Ahmedabad on national holiday, taking an extra science lab; parents and others suspect shoddy construction; photos; map (M)

2001-01-28.title: Experts Try to Make Missile Shield Plan Palatable to China
2001-01-28.abstract: American and Chinese military experts begin exploring ways to make American plans for national missile defense palatable to China and say a compromise may be possible, although it would require near-heretical political steps by leaders in both countries; Pres Bush would have to place clear limits on size of shield and acknowledge China's stature as nuclear power, agreeing, implicitly at least, that China could preserve a capacity to hit back at US with at least a few nuclear bombs, even after suffering a surprise American attack; China would have to move beyond reflexive condemnation and relax hypersecrecy that has surrounded its weapons program, allowing US a clear idea of size and capability of its arsenal, and would have to convince American planners that any nuclear buildup would go only so far and would be keyed to size of American shield (M)

2001-01-29.title: Paris Journal; Contest's Only Potholes Are Political
2001-01-29.abstract: Socialist Party candidate Bertrand Delanoe, hardly household name even six months ago, is frontrunner by healthy margin in election campaign for Mayor of Paris; constant political bickering, odd maneuvers and scent of scandal that have permeated national politics have left many Parisians willing to turn away from conservatives in development Socialists hope augurs well for presidential election in 2002; photo (M)

2001-01-29.title: Maria José, 94, Queen of Italy For Just 27 Days After the War
2001-01-29.abstract: Maria Jose of Savoy, last Queen of Italy who served after World War II for only 27 days before Italians voted to abolish monarchy, dies at age 94; photo (M)

2001-01-29.title: Flare-Up on Kosovo Border Leaves Serb Dead and 4 Hurt
2001-01-29.abstract: Fighting between Yugoslav military and ethnic Albanian guerrillas in tense buffer zone east of Kosovo in southern Serbia rises sharply, with four Serbian soldiers reported wounded and one killed on January 26 (S)

2001-01-29.title: Congo's War Turns a Land Spat Into a Blood Bath
2001-01-29.abstract: Article on dispute over farmland near Bunia, Congo, that has touched off resurgence of ethnic violence between Hema and Lendu groups in which 250 or more people were hacked or shot to death; dispute has become entangled in country's larger two-and-a-half-year-old civil war, which experts say has been raised frightening notch; conditions in region, which has no government, has raised fears of outbreak of violence in other parts of country where order has already been breaking down; map; photos (M)

2001-01-29.title: Upbeat Mood in Manila, and Vows for Future
2001-01-29.abstract: MANILA, Jan. 21 — At church services, shopping malls, restaurants and street stalls across Manila, the mood today was upbeat and the talk centered on the pivotal role played by huge public demonstrations in forcing President Joseph Estrada's departure from office.

2001-01-30.title: A Grisly Mystery in Ukraine Leads to a Government Crisis
2001-01-30.abstract: Political stability in Ukraine is threatened by growing crisis involving Pres Leonid D Kuchma and adbuction and possible murder of journalist Georgy Gongadze; scandal sets off anti-Kuchma demonstrations in dozens of cities, as well as counter demonstrations by state workers ordered into streets or bused to pro-Kuchma rallies; is touched off by discovery of headless body, believed to be Gongadze's, and release of secret recordings made under Kuchma's couch by security agent in which Kuchma appears to order Gongadze's abduction or worse; has touched off fractious brawl in Parliament, where Kuchma's political majority has been upended by defections and formation of new centrist opposition; photo (M)

2001-01-30.title: Judge Reinstates Pinochet Case With New Order for House Arrest
2001-01-30.abstract: Juan Guzman, Chilean investigating judge, reinstates charges against former strongman Gen Augusto Pinochet, signaling he will proceed with human rights case that has reawakened Chile's ghosts; charges Pinochet with being co-conspirator in murders and kidnappings of 75 leftists after coup that brought him to power in 1973; orders that he be placed under house arrest for second time in two months (M)

2001-01-30.title: Mexico Rebel Chief Says the Fight Is Now for Peace
2001-01-30.abstract: Interview with Subcommander Marcos, leader of Zapatista National Liberation Army in Chiapas, Mex, who says pursuit of equal rights for all of country's 10 million indigenous people remain primary goal but that rebels now want peace; maps; photo (M)

2001-01-30.title: U.N. Report Proposes Steps to Fight Global Poverty
2001-01-30.abstract: UNITED NATIONS, Jan. 30 — Declaring that no task was more urgent than rescuing 1 billion people in the world from "abject and dehumanizing poverty," the United Nations today proposed dozens of ways to finance the economic development of poorer countries.

2001-01-30.title: To Fight Sect, China Publicizes a Public Burning
2001-01-30.abstract: BEIJING, Jan. 30 — One week after five people described as Falun Gong members set themselves on fire in Tiananmen Square, China finally informed its own people tonight. It broadcast grisly police videos of the burning believers as part of a newly intensified campaign to discredit the outlawed spiritual group.

2001-01-31.title: Too True, Buildings Kill: Will India Pay Heed Now?
2001-01-31.abstract: India's Jan 26 earthquake draws new attention to 1998 government report that warned that builders are ignoring construction standards that could reduce number of collapsed buildings and loss of life in earthquakes (M)

2001-01-31.title: Mexico Warns of Colombia Drug War Spillover
2001-01-31.abstract: Mexican foreign minister, Jorge G Castaneda, warns Sec of State Colin L Powell in Washington that Colombia's neighbors are extremely concerned about spillover effects of conflict in Colombia; says Mexico will be more 'foreceful' in trying to persuade Colombian rebels, with whom Mexico keeps contact, to negotiate with government of Pres Andres Pastrana (M)

2001-01-31.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2001-01-31.abstract: EUROPE    NORTHERN IRELAND: TRIMBLE FAULTED -- A Belfast High Court judge ruled that the Northern Ireland government's leader, David Trimble, was wrong to bar ministers from Sinn Fein, the political party representing the Irish Republican Army, from attending meetings with their counterparts in the Irish government that were set up by the 1998 peace settlement. Mr. Trimble, who took the action in an effort to produce disarmament moves from the I.R.A., said he would appeal the decision.    Warren Hoge (NYT)

2001-01-31.title: No Crops Spared in Colombia's Coca War
2001-01-31.abstract: Colombian Army says its aggressive land and air assault on country's coca-growing heartland has killed quarter of all coca crops there in last six weeks, but farmers say campaign has destroyed their legal crops like yucca and plantain as well; US officials, who are providing training and financing to eradication effort, confirm army's claim; Colombian health official reports complaints about intoxication, diarrhea, vomiting, skin rashes, red eyes, headaches, especially in children, but American officials dispute such claims, saying numerous tests of the chemical being sprayed, glyphosate, have demonstrated that it cannot harm humans or animals; photos; map (M)

2001-01-31.title: Key Figures in the Lockerbie Trial
2001-01-31.abstract: CAMP ZEIST, Netherlands, Jan. 31 -- Abdel Basset al-Megrahi, one of the two Libyans put on trial for the 1988 Lockerbie airliner bombing, was convicted on Wednesday of the murder of 270 people, but his co-accused was cleared.
